Q: Top-3 highest-grossing restaurants per city
Q: Find customers who abandoned the funnel
OLAP vs OLTP — say this in the interview

lessons.module_name: OLAP engines + streaming — picking the stack

lessons.name: Streaming basics — Kafka, exactly-once, windowing
lessons.module_name: OLAP engines + streaming — picking the stack

cards.a: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD, SUM/AVG OVER (PARTITION BY…). Most SQL screens hide one inside a wordy prompt.

cards.q: NOT IN vs NOT EXISTS — what's the trap?
cards.a: NOT IN returns NULL on any NULL in the subquery (then unknown → no match). NOT EXISTS is safer and equivalent semantically.

cards.q: Composite index (a, b) — which queries use it?
cards.a: Queries filtering on a, or (a + anything), or starting with a in ORDER BY. Queries filtering on b alone CANNOT use it — leftmost-prefix rule.
Each row has valid_from + valid_to (NULL = current) + is_current flag. Updates close the old row (valid_to = now) and insert a new one. Queries filter to the period.
CDC reads the transaction log (Postgres WAL, MySQL binlog) — captures INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. Polling SELECTs miss deletes. CDC scales, polls don't.
(1) Schema (types, non-null). (2) Volume (row count vs avg). (3) Freshness (latest event ≤ N min). (4) Distribution (proportions, ranges). (5) Referential (FKs resolve).
Each task run for date D first DELETEs WHERE date=D, then inserts. Safe to re-run. Never APPEND without a partition key — backfills will duplicate.
SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY group ORDER BY date). GROUP BY collapses; you'd lose per-row detail.
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY group ORDER BY metric DESC) → filter rn ≤ N in an outer query.
LAG(col, n): previous nth row's value. LEAD: next. Useful for diffs, deltas, conversion funnels.

cards.q: Composite index on (a, b, c) — usable for WHERE b=X?
cards.a: NO. Leftmost-prefix rule: must use leftmost column (a). WHERE a=X, (a, b), (a, b, c) all work; skipping a doesn't.
Index that contains all columns needed by query (key + INCLUDE columns). DB satisfies query from index alone; no table fetch.
UPDATE/DELETE leave dead tuples (multi-version concurrency). VACUUM reclaims dead-tuple space + ANALYZE updates planner statistics.
OLTP: row-oriented, single-record write/read, normalized (Postgres, MySQL). OLAP: columnar, batch aggregates, denormalized (Snowflake, ClickHouse).
Star: fact + denormalized dim tables. Fewer joins, faster queries. Snowflake: normalized dims (more joins). Star = analytical default.

cards.q: MERGE / UPSERT pattern for idempotent loads?
cards.a: MERGE INTO target USING source ON keys WHEN MATCHED UPDATE WHEN NOT MATCHED INSERT. Postgres uses INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (key) DO UPDATE. Combined with a stable natural key, re-runs converge to the same state.

cards.q: Analytics on a 1TB Postgres table — what to do?
cards.a: Move the analytics workload to a columnar engine: Snowflake / ClickHouse / DuckDB / BigQuery. Row-store Postgres is poor at full-table aggregate scans. If you must stay in-Postgres, extensions like pg_duckdb or pg_mooncake bolt a columnar engine onto Postgres.
Bronze (raw) → Silver (cleaned/normalized) → Gold (business-ready aggregates). Standard layer model for Delta Lake / Iceberg.
Avro/Protobuf schemas tracked centrally with backward/forward compat rules. Lets producers + consumers evolve independently.

cards.q: Iceberg vs Delta vs Hudi — what's the 2026 state?
cards.a: Three open table formats over Parquet adding ACID, time travel, and schema evolution to data lakes. Iceberg has won industry consolidation (Databricks acquired Tabular 2024; AWS S3 Tables, Snowflake, BigQuery all support it natively). Delta still leads inside Databricks/Spark; Hudi specializes in record-level upserts. Cross-format projects (Delta UniForm, Apache XTable) blur the lines.

cards.q: RANK vs DENSE_RANK vs ROW_NUMBER on a tie?
cards.a: Two rows tied at position 2: ROW_NUMBER gives 1,2,3,4 (arbitrary tiebreak). RANK gives 1,2,2,4 (skips 3). DENSE_RANK gives 1,2,2,3 (no gap).
Divides the ordered partition into 4 roughly equal buckets and labels each row 1..4. Standard tool for quartiles, deciles (NTILE(10)), or percentile bands.

ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW counts physical rows. RANGE counts logical values of the ORDER BY column (ties grouped). Default is RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING TO CURRENT ROW -- subtle bug source on duplicate timestamps.

cards.q: Deduplicate rows keeping the latest version?
cards.a: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY natural_key ORDER BY updated_at DESC) AS rn, then WHERE rn = 1 in an outer query/CTE. Works for CDC streams and replayed events.

cards.q: Antijoin: rows in A with no match in B?
cards.a: LEFT JOIN B ON ... WHERE B.id IS NULL, or NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM B WHERE ...). NOT EXISTS is NULL-safe; NOT IN breaks on any NULL in B's column.

cards.q: Why does a LEFT JOIN inflate row counts?
cards.a: The right table has multiple matching rows per left key, so each left row fans out. Fix: pre-aggregate the right side in a CTE, or join on a unique key, or use DISTINCT ON / window-then-filter.

cards.q: CTE vs subquery vs temp table?
cards.a: Subquery / CTE = inline, planner usually inlines them (Postgres 12+ inlines non-recursive CTEs). Temp table = materialized, stats collected, reused across queries. Use temp tables when the intermediate is large and reused.

cards.q: B-tree vs hash vs GIN vs BRIN -- when to pick each?
cards.a: B-tree: equality + range, default. Hash: equality only, rarely better than B-tree. GIN: multi-value columns (arrays, jsonb, full-text). BRIN: huge tables with physical-order correlation (time-series append-only), tiny footprint.

cards.q: Index selectivity -- why does it matter?
cards.a: Selectivity = distinct values / rows. Low-selectivity columns (e.g. boolean is_active) make indexes useless because the planner correctly chooses a seq scan. Index high-cardinality columns or use partial/covering indexes.

EXPLAIN shows the planner's estimated plan + costs without executing. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually runs the query and prints real row counts and timings -- the only way to spot estimate-vs-actual skew that drives bad plans.
Code fetches a list of N parents, then issues one child query per parent (N+1 total round trips). Fix: a single JOIN, an IN (...) batch fetch, or ORM eager loading (e.g. SQLAlchemy selectinload).

cards.q: GROUP BY vs HAVING vs WHERE?
cards.a: WHERE filters rows before grouping. GROUP BY collapses rows by key. HAVING filters groups after aggregation. You cannot reference an aggregate in WHERE, only HAVING.

cards.q: ROLLUP vs CUBE vs GROUPING SETS?
cards.a: ROLLUP (a, b, c): hierarchical subtotals + grand total. CUBE: every subset (2^n combinations). GROUPING SETS: explicit list of grouping combos. All emit one result set in a single pass instead of UNION ALL.

cards.q: Columnar storage -- why fast for analytics?
cards.a: Each column stored contiguously, so SELECT SUM(amount) reads only that column's pages. Adjacent values compress well (RLE, dictionary). Row stores must read every column of every row -- huge IO waste for wide tables.

Partitioning: one DB splits a table into segments by key (date, hash) for prune-on-scan; same node. Sharding: data split across multiple nodes/databases for horizontal scale. Partition first, shard only when one node can't hold it.

Inside the same DB transaction, write the business row AND an outbox row. A separate process tails the outbox (or via CDC) and publishes to Kafka. Guarantees the event ships iff the transaction commits -- no dual-write race.

Lambda: parallel batch + speed (streaming) layers, merged at query time -- duplicates logic. Kappa: a single streaming pipeline; reprocess history by replaying the log. Modern stacks (Flink, Materialize) favor Kappa.
staging (1:1 cleanup of source tables) -> intermediate (joins, business logic) -> marts (fact + dim tables consumed by BI). Tests and docs live alongside models; refs build the DAG.

Type 1: overwrite -- no history. Type 2: new row per change with valid_from/valid_to -- full history (default for analytics). Type 4: keep current row in main table + a separate history table for changes.

cards.q: Snowflake vs BigQuery vs Redshift -- 2026 tradeoff?
cards.a: Snowflake: separated storage/compute, per-warehouse scaling, multi-cloud, strong governance. BigQuery: serverless, slot-based pricing, deepest GCP + ML integration. Redshift: AWS-native, RA3 separates storage, cheapest at steady high utilization but more tuning.
